verse_specific_anchors: the wind-boat metaphor (vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi) is preserved verbatim structurally — 'the boat is not weak — the boat is moving with intent. The wind has only to enter the sail' — this metaphor is unique to BG 2.67 in the Gita; no other verse uses the wind-boat image

the specific causal chain (un-restrained sense → un-yoked mind → drawn-along consciousness → eroded prajñā) is anchored in 2.67's specific Sanskrit structure: indriyāṇāṁ caratām (wandering senses) → mano 'nuvidhīyate (mind led along) → harati prajñām (steals prajñā). No other verse runs this specific chain

the explicit framing 'one un-restrained input stream is enough' is the verse's specific structural claim — even one sense (yan, singular) drawn behind is sufficient. This contrasts with verses that name plural sense-control as the discipline (e.g. 2.58, 2.60, 2.61, 2.68); 2.67 specifically names the singular-suffices case
objection: The analog could not equally apply to 2.58 (the tortoise verse — sense-withdrawal), 2.60 (the senses violently carry off the mind even of the wise), 2.61 (the disciplined yogi keeps the senses subdued), or 2.68 (positive corollary — sense-restraint = established prajñā). Each of those verses has its own metaphor (tortoise / violent carrying / yogi seated / restraint sarvaśaḥ). 2.67's wind-boat metaphor and singular-suffices framing are unique. Pass.
